title: SUMAYA Care 360 - Complete Hospital Platform
narration: Welcome to SUMAYA Care 360, a multi-tenant hospital, clinic, and virtual-care operations platform. This walkthrough uses a live demonstration tenant with realistic patient, clinical, diagnostic, inpatient, finance, and governance data. We will follow the complete patient journey and then examine every major module, report, security boundary, and role-based workspace.

title: Role-aware hospital dashboard
narration: The hospital dashboard is role aware. Each card is calculated from live tenant-scoped transactions and opens the corresponding operational worklist. Administrators see the whole hospital: patients, queues, emergency cases, inpatient census, diagnostics, pharmacy, claims, invoices, beds, and module work. Doctors, nurses, reception staff, finance users, and technical staff receive smaller dashboards appropriate to their responsibilities.

title: Patient administration command center
narration: Patient administration is the operational center of the platform. It consolidates registration, appointments, check-in, consultation, emergency, admission, diagnostic work, nursing tasks, discharge, and outstanding billing into one patient-centered board. The stage filters show where every patient is now, while the next-action column tells staff exactly what must happen next. Pending orders, care tasks, beds, and balances are visible without reconstructing the story across separate modules.

title: Longitudinal Patient 360 record
narration: Selecting a patient opens the longitudinal Patient 360 panel. Identity, MRN, demographics, contact information, and blood group stay in context while the timeline combines appointments, emergency events, encounters, inpatient stays, laboratory orders, radiology, pharmacy dispensing, nursing tasks, and invoices. This is the shared operational history needed for safe handoffs and continuity of care, while tenant isolation and audit controls protect the record.

title: Patient registration and CRM
narration: Patient registration creates the master patient record and medical record number. Staff capture substantial demographics, identifiers, contact information, address, blood group, national identification, emergency contact, branch assignment, and status. Search, sorting, pagination, and export support front-office work at scale. Existing patients can be found before creating duplicates, and every clinical and financial transaction links back to this tenant-scoped patient identity.

title: Providers, specialties, and schedules
narration: Provider management maintains doctors and other care professionals, including provider codes, specialties, licenses, branch assignments, consultation tariffs, schedules, slot lengths, and supported visit modes. Appointment booking uses these database-driven provider records instead of hard-coded lists. Administrators can keep availability current while clinical users see only the providers relevant to their assigned organization and role.

title: Appointments, arrival, and queue management
narration: Appointments cover scheduled visits, walk-ins, in-person consultations, and telemedicine. The worklist includes patient, provider, visit reason, scheduled time, mode, status, and queue token. Reception teams can book, confirm, check in, cancel, or record a no-show. Check-in becomes the formal handoff to vitals and consultation, preserving an auditable progression from arrival through clinical care.

title: Emergency arrival and ESI triage
narration: The emergency board supports rapid registration and Emergency Severity Index triage from level one through five. Staff record the chief complaint, acuity, clinical notes, and current state. Cases move from arrived to triaged, treatment, and disposition. The disposition can discharge, transfer, record a patient leaving without being seen, or hand the patient to inpatient admission, preserving the emergency story for downstream care.

title: End-to-end outpatient care journey
narration: The care journey guides staff through a real outpatient visit: patient selection, appointment or walk-in, encounter start, vitals, clinical documentation, diagnosis, prescription, discharge plan, invoice, and payment. Each step writes to the same database used by the specialist modules. The result is not a mock wizard; it is a coordinated entry point into the patient, appointment, encounter, medication, billing, payment, and audit records.

title: Clinical encounters and medical documentation
narration: Clinical encounters hold the consultation itself. The record connects the patient, provider, appointment, encounter type, chief complaint, assessment, plan, start time, closure, vitals, notes, diagnoses, and prescription. Structured disease and medicine masters reduce free-text inconsistency. Open encounters remain on the clinical worklist until the care plan and required documentation are complete.

title: Telemedicine and virtual waiting room
narration: Telemedicine uses a provider abstraction so each tenant can configure its supported video service. The workflow includes a booked tele-slot, identity verification, consent, virtual waiting room, secure room tokens, admission by the clinician, in-call notes, prescription, post-call summary, and completion. Recording metadata is unavailable until versioned consent is captured, and payment data remains tokenized rather than storing raw card details.

title: Inpatient admission and bed allocation
narration: Inpatient administration starts with a patient and an available bed from the facilities master. It records the admission number, ward, bed, diagnosis, admission status, and admission time. Bed status changes to occupied as part of the transaction. The active census connects with nursing, diagnostics, procedures, pharmacy, billing, and claims. Discharge releases the bed and creates the appropriate inpatient financial record.

title: Nursing worklist and care tasks
narration: The nursing module turns inpatient and clinical plans into an accountable task worklist. Tasks can represent vitals, medication administration, wound care, observations, and shift handover. Each item links to the patient and optional admission, includes assignment and due time, and progresses from pending to in progress and completed. The dashboard and reports show outstanding workload so care is not lost between teams or shifts.

title: Operation theatre and procedure management
narration: Operation theatre management tracks the surgical journey from request and scheduling through pre-operative checks, procedure, and completion. Cases include patient, procedure code and name, surgeon, theatre, scheduled time, status, safety checklist, intra-operative notes, and implant tracking. This gives clinical, inventory, billing, and governance teams a shared procedural record while maintaining the patient-centered timeline.

title: Laboratory order-to-result workflow
narration: Laboratory diagnostics begins with a clinician order against the lab-test master. The order receives a reference number and moves through ordered, sample collected, result entered, verified, or critical alert states. Results, notes, structured values, and critical flags remain linked to the patient and encounter. Search, filters, pagination, and export support the laboratory worklist, while critical results are visible in operational reporting.

title: Radiology, reporting, and PACS links
narration: Radiology manages imaging orders from request through scheduling, acquisition, reporting, and critical result handling. Each study links to the patient, encounter, provider, study code, appointment time, report text, and optional PACS reference. Status transitions are validated so work cannot jump unpredictably between stages. The radiology dashboard separates pending acquisition from studies awaiting a report and completed results.

title: Prescription validation and dispensing
narration: The pharmacy queue receives prescriptions created in encounters and teleconsultations. Pharmacists can review medicine, dose, frequency, duration, instructions, quantity, substitution, stock context, and dispense status. Dispensing can occur line by line or from the entire prescription. The workflow distinguishes queued, prepared, dispensed, and exceptional cases, creating traceability between the clinician order, pharmacy action, patient, encounter, and inventory.

title: Rooms, beds, and facilities readiness
narration: Rooms and facilities configuration supplies the operational foundation for inpatient care. Room categories, tariff classes, nursing stations, room codes, beds, isolation flags, equipment tags, and availability are maintained by tenant and branch. Admission uses this live inventory, occupancy reporting reads the same bed states, and discharge returns the bed to available status. This prevents separate spreadsheets from disagreeing with the patient census.

title: Billing, invoices, and tokenized payments
narration: Billing creates estimates and invoices from configured tariffs rather than arbitrary values. Invoice lines record service, quantity, unit price, amount, tax, total, status, patient, and encounter. Payments store gateway references and masked details only; raw card numbers are not accepted. Finance teams can follow issued, partially paid, paid, cancelled, and outstanding items while audit events preserve the payment lifecycle.

title: Insurance eligibility, pre-authorization, and claims
narration: Insurance and claims administration connects patients to payer masters, policies, pre-authorization numbers, claim amounts, and supporting notes. Claims progress through controlled states from draft and submission to review, approval, settlement, rejection, or rework. The claims report summarizes open exposure and claimed value, while the patient timeline and revenue cycle provide clinical and financial context without duplicating the claim record.

title: Revenue cycle management
narration: Revenue cycle management joins eligibility, estimates, authorization, charge capture, invoicing, payment, insurance submission, denial follow-up, and settlement. It gives finance leaders a cross-module view rather than treating billing and claims as unrelated desks. Operational status, accountable next steps, payer information, and exported reports support daily follow-up and month-end analysis.

title: Inventory, procurement, and stores
narration: Inventory and procurement manages stock requests, approval, purchase ordering, receiving, storage, and closure. Dedicated lifecycle rules prevent a draft request from becoming received without the required intermediate decisions. Records capture the submodule, reference, accountable status, patient or provider context where relevant, and flexible operational details. Pharmacy, surgery, wards, and facilities can therefore share an auditable supply workflow.

title: Disease and care pathways
narration: Care pathways convert a disease program into defined milestones and measurable follow-up. Administrators configure pathway templates against disease codes, then enroll patients, record the current milestone, pause or resume participation, and complete or withdraw the program through valid transitions. This supports longitudinal care beyond a single visit and provides outcome-oriented reporting for care managers.

title: Chronic disease programs
narration: Chronic disease programs maintain recurring care for patients who need monitoring over months or years. Enrollment, active management, review-due status, interventions, and completion form a controlled lifecycle. The module can hold disease-specific goals, review plans, monitoring details, and patient context while the patient record continues to show consultations, diagnostics, medicines, and follow-up activity.

title: Physiotherapy and rehabilitation
narration: Physiotherapy and rehabilitation follows assessment, plan creation, scheduled sessions, in-session documentation, progress, and completion. Records can remain tied to the referring patient and provider, with detailed program payloads supporting goals, exercises, precautions, and outcomes. This makes rehabilitation part of the overall care journey rather than an isolated appointment calendar.

title: Discharge and post-treatment continuity
narration: Post-treatment care begins before discharge is complete. The lifecycle supports a documented plan, scheduled follow-up, active monitoring, escalation, and closure. It is intended for discharge instructions, medication reconciliation, follow-up appointments, home monitoring, red-flag education, and escalation back to clinical care. The discharge report identifies patients whose follow-up work has not yet been created.

title: Women, child, and specialty care
narration: Women, child, and specialty care provides a dedicated lifecycle for programs such as antenatal care, pediatrics, and specialty clinics. Registration, scheduling, visit activity, follow-up, and closure are tracked with patient and provider context. Configurable submodules and detailed data fields allow the tenant to model specialty requirements while retaining the common security, audit, table, workflow, and export standards.

title: Ambulance and patient transport
narration: Ambulance and transport records move from request to dispatch, en route, on scene, transporting, handover, and closure. Each transition is controlled and audited. The record can carry patient details, origin, destination, urgency, vehicle, crew, timestamps, and handover information. Location services can be connected only under the configured purpose and consent policy.

title: Diet, catering, and housekeeping
narration: Diet, catering, and housekeeping coordinate essential non-clinical services around inpatient care. Requests move from ordered to preparing, delivered, and closed, with submodules for meal planning, dietary restrictions, cleaning, room turnover, and service completion. Tenant-scoped worklists and status reporting make these services visible to hospital operations instead of leaving them in phone calls and paper registers.

title: Documents, consent, and clinical forms
narration: Document management stores metadata for clinical forms, consent, reports, images, and other patient or administrative files using tenant-scoped storage keys. Documents remain associated with their entity, content type, status, branch, and audit history. Clinical templates and consent templates come from master configuration, supporting consistent capture and future signed-document workflows without embedding files directly in operational tables.

title: Notifications and patient engagement
narration: Notifications use configured templates and an outbox rather than sending messages invisibly inside clinical transactions. Email, SMS, and other channels record the recipient, subject, body, delivery status, creation time, and sent time. Appointments, diagnostics, telemedicine, billing, and follow-up workflows can enqueue communication while workers and audit logs provide operational traceability.

title: Patient portal experience
narration: The patient portal is a restricted self-service workspace. Patients can see their own appointments, clinical summaries, prescriptions, invoices, teleconsultation access, and relevant documents without receiving the hospital-wide menus available to staff. Route allowlists and tenant-aware authentication enforce the separation, while the same underlying records prevent portal information from drifting away from staff workflows.

title: Consent-gated location services
narration: Location services are purpose based and consent gated. The tenant configures permitted purposes such as telemedicine check-in, home care, or transport. A location event records the purpose, patient, coordinates, accuracy, consent reference, and capture time. If the configured purpose requires consent, the API rejects location capture without it, preserving privacy and auditability.

title: Integrations and interoperability
narration: Integration management provides a controlled lifecycle for external connections such as laboratory systems, payments, messaging, accounting, FHIR, HL7, or PACS. Connections progress from draft to testing, active, failed, or suspended. The platform keeps endpoint configuration, testing evidence, status, and operational ownership visible while secrets remain references to secure configuration rather than values displayed in the interface.

title: Data governance and platform operations
narration: Data governance coordinates proposed, approved, active, and archived platform policies. It supports operational records for data ownership, retention, quality, masking, access, backups, recovery, and compliance reviews. These records complement immutable audit events, API logs, role-based permissions, and tenant isolation, giving administrators an accountable governance program rather than a static policy document.

title: Provider marketplace and onboarding
narration: The provider marketplace tracks external or network providers through onboarding, contracting, activation, suspension, and termination. Profiles can include specialties, service areas, commercial terms, credential status, and supporting details. Controlled transitions preserve governance while allowing the organization to extend its care network without mixing marketplace onboarding with the internal provider directory.

title: Mobile application release operations
narration: Mobile application operations track releases from draft through beta, released, and deprecated states. The module can hold platform, version, audience, rollout notes, feature flags, and release evidence. Patient, doctor, nurse, and other mobile experiences are expected to use the same secured APIs and tenant-scoped workflows as the web platform, protecting parity between channels.

title: Hospital and tenant administration
narration: Hospital administration configures branches, departments, branding, operational settings, and the structures used by clinical work. Tenant administrators can maintain the organization without crossing into another tenant. Branches and departments feed provider assignments, patient registration, appointments, beds, tariffs, reporting filters, and audit context, so administrative configuration directly supports the care workflow.

title: Identity, roles, MFA, and access control
narration: Identity and security use authenticated sessions, database roles, permissions, explicit route allowlists, and tenant context. Administrative, clinical, front-office, finance, pharmacy, laboratory, radiology, and patient roles receive distinct screens and actions. Multi-factor authentication hooks, session handling, password policy, and audit events support stronger access control, while cross-tenant requests are rejected.

title: Database-driven hospital masters
narration: Master data removes hard-coded clinical and commercial values from the application. Countries, genders, specialties, diseases, medicines, laboratory tests, tariffs, payers, room categories, beds, templates, consent purposes, video providers, and UI actions are maintained as governed records. Dropdowns and transaction validation use these masters, improving consistency and allowing each tenant to configure its real hospital operations.

title: Clinical, administrative, and API audit trail
narration: Audit and governance capture who performed an action, the entity affected, old and new values where appropriate, time, correlation identifier, and tenant context. Separate views cover business actions, API calls, and sensitive clinical access. Payment, prescription, consent, telemedicine, location, master-data, and role changes are designed to remain traceable while protected fields are masked.

title: Seventeen live operational reports
narration: The reporting center contains seventeen tenant-scoped reports grouped for leadership, patient administration, clinical operations, inpatient care, diagnostics, finance, care management, and governance. Date filters apply to live transactional queries. Each report presents key metrics and detailed rows, drills back into its source module, and exports to Excel, CSV, JSON, or a print-ready PDF. This scene runs the executive report to demonstrate the live result panel.

title: Complete module map and feature coverage
narration: The module map is the complete catalog of the platform. It organizes all thirty-six modules by the patient and hospital operating flow, shows active work counts, and provides direct entry into each dedicated desk. Feature coverage is tracked against the enterprise requirements catalogue, making gaps visible instead of hiding unfinished work behind menu labels.

title: Doctor workspace
narration: The doctor workspace proves role separation. Clinical dashboards, patients, appointments, encounters, telemedicine, diagnostics, pharmacy visibility, and the patient administration board are available, while tenant configuration and unrelated administrative operations remain hidden. The same restriction is enforced when a user attempts to enter a disallowed URL directly.

title: Nurse workspace
narration: The nurse workspace centers on the patient board, encounters, inpatient census, nursing tasks, and MFA settings. Finance, claims administration, pharmacy control, and tenant configuration are absent. This keeps navigation focused and supports least-privilege access while still giving nurses the patient context needed for safe bedside care.

title: Reception workspace
narration: Reception staff receive the front-office workflow: the patient command center, registration, providers, appointments, queue management, and emergency arrival. They can coordinate entry into care without receiving broad clinical, financial, or platform-administration access. This role is the starting point for scheduled visits, walk-ins, and rapid emergency registration.

title: Billing and insurance workspace
narration: Billing staff receive patients for financial context, billing, insurance claims, revenue cycle, and authorized reports. Clinical documentation and hospital configuration are excluded. Finance users can follow estimates, invoices, payments, outstanding value, claims, and settlements while tokenized payment handling and audit events support PCI-aware operations.

title: Patient self-service workspace
narration: The patient role opens directly into the self-service portal and cannot browse hospital-wide patient lists or staff modules. This demonstrates the strictest view of role-based access: the patient sees only personal care information and account actions permitted by the portal. Staff workflows remain inaccessible even when their URLs are known.

title: One connected hospital workflow
narration: SUMAYA Care 360 now presents one connected operational story: register the patient, coordinate arrival, assess and treat, manage diagnostics and medicines, admit when necessary, support nursing and procedures, complete discharge, collect payment or submit a claim, and continue follow-up. Every module shares tenant isolation, role control, workflow states, search, tables, reporting, export, and audit standards. This concludes the complete feature walkthrough.
